food_subgroup: Processed poultry — dry-fried spiced chicken floss

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for serunding ayam (Malay spiced chicken floss). Mapped to broader chicken food product class. A specific term for dry-fried spiced chicken floss does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to serunding ayam. Mapped to processed poultry meat products as the closest defensible category. Serunding is a dry-fried spiced shredded meat preparation not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for serunding ayam or equivalent Malay spiced chicken floss preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of serunding ayam is approximately 30 grams, equivalent to about 2 heaped tablespoons, commonly served as a side accompaniment to lemang, ketupat, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — Poultry and Poultry Products; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked) for fatty acid fractions
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for serunding ayam exists in MyFCD. Values are adapted from MyFCD poultry entries and USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken data, adjusted for the dry-frying process with coconut milk and kerisik, which concentrates protein and fat and reduces moisture. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk and kerisik contribution. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat fractions set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this preparation is not available in either source. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with adapted source estimates and are retained with source adaptation noted.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — analogous estimate based on high-protein, high-fat spiced meat preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for serunding ayam in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous high-protein, high-fat spiced meat preparations. The high protein (26.5g/100g) and fat (24.8g/100g) content significantly attenuates glycemic response. Carbohydrate content is moderate (10.5g/100g) but largely offset by protein and fat. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: (35 × 3.15g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.1, rounded to 1.2. This is consistent with low-GL classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100g; 144 mg per 30g serving) from added salt and seasoning, combined with significant saturated fat from coconut milk and kerisik
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit serunding ayam to small portions (≤30g) and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, as the sodium content of 480 mg per 100g can contribute substantially to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI ~35) driven by high protein and fat content, with moderate carbohydrate (10.5g/100g) and sugar (5.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Serunding ayam is generally suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions, as its high protein and fat content attenuates postprandial glycemic response; however, commercial versions may contain added sugar and should be checked for carbohydrate content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat content (estimated ~14.5g/100g) from coconut milk and kerisik, alongside high sodium (480 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume serunding ayam sparingly due to its high saturated fat content from coconut-derived ingredients, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; portion control to 30g or less per occasion is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric, ginger, and galangal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, galangal) in serunding ayam may offer modest benefit for rheumatoid arthritis; however, the high-heat dry-frying process may reduce bioavailability of heat-sensitive phytochemicals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Serunding Ayam is a traditional Malay spiced chicken floss originating from Malaysia, widely prepared for festive occasions such as Eid al-Fitr (Hari Raya Aidilfitri). The dish is made by slow-cooking chicken breast or thigh in a rich mixture of coconut milk and kerisik (toasted grated coconut paste) together with a complex spice paste comprising lemongrass, galangal, ginger, turmeric, coriander, and chili. Once the chicken is tender, it is shredded and then dry-fried in a wok over low heat for several hours until it achieves a characteristically dry, light, and fluffy fibrous texture resembling floss. The result is a deeply savory, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet condiment with an intense concentrated flavor. Nutritionally, serunding ayam is high in protein (approximately 26.5g per 100g) and moderate in fat (approximately 24.8g per 100g), predominantly saturated fat from coconut-derived ingredients. Its carbohydrate content is low to moderate (approximately 10.5g per 100g), giving it a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35. The sodium content is high at approximately 480 mg per 100g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and halal. Serunding ayam is typically served as an accompaniment to lemang (glutinous rice cooked in bamboo), ketupat (compressed rice cake), or plain steamed rice. A standard serving is approximately 30 grams.
common_user_queries: How many calories in serunding ayam?
Is serunding ayam suitable for diabetics?
Is serunding ayam high in sodium?
What spices are in serunding ayam?
What do you eat serunding ayam with?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for serunding ayam exists in MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from MyFCD poultry entries and USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken data, adjusted for the dry-frying and coconut milk concentration process. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with these adapted estimates. GI is an analogous estimate with no direct measurement available. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for serunding ayam prepared by traditional slow-cooking and dry-frying with coconut milk and kerisik are not available in MyFCD (IMR Malaysia) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. Saturated fat estimated at 14.5g/100g based on coconut milk and kerisik contribution to total fat. All core macros (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dry-fried spiced chicken preparation. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to high-protein, high-fat spiced meat preparations; no direct GI measurement for serunding ayam exists in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as approximately 1.2 (low). Input source listed as Malaysian Journal of Nutrition (nutriweb.org.my) has been replaced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary sources per Project Veritas source policy.
